subject: ap-chemistry
theme: Chemical reactions transform matter through four major types — precipitation, acid-base neutralization, oxidation-reduction, and combustion — each governed by conservation of mass and charge.

core_concepts.title: Types of Chemical Reactions
core_concepts.detail: Synthesis: A + B → AB. Decomposition: AB → A + B. Single displacement: A + BC → AC + B (redox). Double displacement: AB + CD → AD + CB (precipitation, neutralization). Combustion: hydrocarbon + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O. All reactions must be balanced (mass and charge conservation).

core_concepts.title: Net Ionic Equations
core_concepts.detail: Net ionic equations remove spectator ions and show only the species that change. Steps: (1) write balanced molecular equation; (2) split strong electrolytes into ions; (3) cancel spectator ions. Spectator ions appear unchanged on both sides.

core_concepts.title: Acid-Base Reactions and Brønsted-Lowry Theory
core_concepts.detail: A Brønsted-Lowry acid donates H⁺; a base accepts H⁺. When an acid donates H⁺, it forms its conjugate base (which differs by one H⁺). Neutralization of a strong acid and strong base produces a neutral salt + water. Titration reaches the equivalence point when moles of acid equal moles of base (adjusted for stoichiometry).

core_concepts.title: Stoichiometry and Limiting Reagents
core_concepts.detail: Convert all reactant masses to moles, use the balanced equation to find which runs out first (limiting reagent), then calculate product moles and convert to mass. Theoretical yield is the calculated maximum; percent yield = (actual/theoretical) × 100%.

core_concepts.title: Oxidation States and Redox Reactions
core_concepts.detail: Oxidation state rules: free elements = 0; O = $-2$ (usually); H = $+1$ (usually); sum = charge of ion/molecule. In redox reactions, oxidation = increase in oxidation state (loss of electrons); reduction = decrease in oxidation state (gain of electrons). Half-reactions show either oxidation or reduction separately and are balanced by adding $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{H}^+$, and electrons.

core_concepts.title: Solubility Rules and Precipitation
core_concepts.detail: All nitrates, Group IA, and ammonium salts are soluble. Most chlorides, sulfates are soluble with exceptions (AgCl, BaSO₄, PbSO₄). Most carbonates, phosphates, hydroxides, and sulfides are insoluble with Group IA/NH₄⁺ exceptions. Knowing these allows prediction of whether a precipitate forms.

formulas.name: Percent yield
formulas.description: Compares the experimentally obtained product mass to the theoretically calculated maximum, expressed as a percentage.
formulas.example: If theoretical yield = 25.0 g and actual yield = 21.3 g: $\% \text{yield} = (21.3/25.0) \times 100\% = 85.2\%$.

formulas.name: Titration stoichiometry
formulas.katex_string: n_a \cdot M_a \cdot V_a = n_b \cdot M_b \cdot V_b
formulas.description: At the equivalence point, moles of H⁺ equal moles of OH⁻, adjusted by the number of exchangeable protons ($n_a$, $n_b$) for polyprotic acids/bases.
formulas.example: To find $M_a$ of $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ neutralized by 40.0 mL of 0.200 M NaOH: $(2)(M_a)(25.0) = (1)(0.200)(40.0)$; $M_a = 0.160$ mol/L.

exam_tip: Oxidation state calculations are frequently tested. Watch for periodic exceptions: H is +1 except in metal hydrides (−1); O is −2 except in peroxides (−1) and $\text{OF}_2$ (+2). In balancing redox reactions using half-reactions, always balance O with H₂O, then H with H⁺ (acidic) or OH⁻ (basic), then balance charge with electrons.
